epigraph.text: Iron sharpens iron, and one man sharpens another.
epigraph.quote: Correlation is the most powerful tool for prediction that statisticians possess.

content.text:
How do we measure the strength and direction of the linear relationship 
between two random variables?

items: $\Cov(X, Y) > 0$: $X$ and $Y$ tend to increase together
$\Cov(X, Y) < 0$: when $X$ increases, $Y$ tends to decrease
$\Cov(X, Y) = 0$: no linear association (but may still be related!)

content.title: Properties of Covariance
content.content:
1. $\Cov(X, X) = \Var(X)$

2. $\Cov(X, Y) = \Cov(Y, X)$

3. $\Cov(aX + b, cY + d) = ac \cdot \Cov(X, Y)$

4. $\Var(X + Y) = \Var(X) + \Var(Y) + 2\Cov(X, Y)$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Correlation
content.content:
The **correlation** (or Pearson correlation 
coefficient) is the standardized covariance:
\[
 \rho_{XY} = \Corr(X, Y) = \frac{\Cov(X, Y)}{\sigma_X \sigma_Y}
\]

content.title: Properties of Correlation
content.content:
1. $-1 \leq \rho \leq 1$

2. $|\rho| = 1$ if and only if $Y = aX + b$ for some constants

3. $\rho = 0$ does not imply independence (see caveat below)

4. Correlation is unitless
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Correlation Measures Linear Association Only
content.content:
$\rho = 0$ means no *linear* relationship. Variables can have 
strong nonlinear relationships yet have $\rho = 0$. For example, if 
$Y = X^2$ and $X$ is symmetric about 0, then $\Cov(X, Y) = 0$.
